description: A pricing structure is discussed across multiple sessions with different proposals. The final decision is made in a short session. Probe asks for current pricing. Tests whether the system returns the resolved decision rather than the most-discussed option.

messages.content: We're rethinking pricing. Current plan: $29/month for Pro, $99/month for Team. The growth team is pushing for $19/month Pro to increase conversion.
Noted. Current pricing is $29/mo Pro, $99/mo Team. Growth team proposes dropping Pro to $19/mo.
Had a long debate about the $19 price point. Finance says it kills margins. We modeled $24/month as a compromise and $49/month for a new Business tier between Pro and Team.
Updated. Compromise model under discussion: $24/mo Pro, new $49/mo Business tier, $99/mo Team.
More pricing discussions. We ran an A/B test and the $19 price point actually had worse conversion than $29 because of perceived quality concerns. The team is leaning back toward keeping $29 for Pro.
Interesting. A/B test showed $19 hurt conversion vs $29. Team leaning toward keeping $29 Pro price.

Final pricing decision: we're keeping $29/month for Pro, adding a $59/month Business tier with team features, and raising Team to $119/month with SSO and audit logs. Effective April 1st for new customers.
Final pricing locked in. Pro $29/mo, Business $59/mo (new), Team $119/mo. Effective April 1st for new customers.
A prospect just asked about our pricing tiers. What are our current prices?

eval_rubric.notes: System must return the final approved pricing from session 4, not any of the earlier proposals ($19, $24, $49) or the original Team price ($99). Returning a draft price as current is a false closure failure.

interference.description: The $19 and $24 price points were discussed at greater length across sessions 1-3 than the final decision in session 4. The $19 proposal in particular is mentioned in two sessions. Systems biased toward frequently-discussed or recently-retrieved facts may surface draft prices.
